summary: I was born and raised in Frankfort, Kentucky, growing up on a hobby farm on the rural west side of Franklin County. Frankfort, as the state capital, has always been a place where I felt deeply connected to community and nature, especially the creeks and river that inspire my art. My early experiences here shaped my identity as an artist and activist.

I grew up in Franklin County, specifically on the rural west side near Frankfort, where I explored the farm and natural surroundings as a child. The geography and ecology of Franklin County, with its green spaces and waterways, have been a constant source of inspiration for me both personally and artistically. My roots here are strong, and I returned to this area after spending time away.

During high school, I attended every I Love Mountains Day I could, which was pivotal in shaping my early activism. It was a way for me to plug into community initiatives around environmental justice right here in Kentucky. Those experiences helped me identify as an activist from a young age.

In college, I worked with the Backside Learning Center running after-school programs for immigrant and migrant youth in Boyle County. This was part of my effort to build community through arts and music, connecting with diverse populations in the area. Boyle County was an important place for me to engage in meaningful community work.

Alongside Boyle County, I also worked in Mercer County through the Backside Learning Center to support immigrant and migrant youth with arts and music programs. This experience deepened my connection to Kentucky's diverse cultures and helped me understand the challenges these communities face. Mercer County was a key location for my community-building efforts during college.

The Backside Learning Center was a nonprofit I worked with during college, where I ran after-school programs for immigrant and migrant youth in Boyle and Mercer counties. I used arts and music as tools to build community and support these young people. It was a formative experience that combined my passions for education, culture, and social justice.

I interned at the Backside Learning Center located at Churchill Downs, where I worked with migrant workers and their families. This internship gave me insight into the lives of migrant communities connected to the horse racing industry. Churchill Downs was a unique setting for combining my interests in community work and cultural engagement.

Kentucky is my home state, where I was born, raised, and have returned to live and work. I deeply appreciate Kentucky's cultural diversity and natural environment, which have influenced my art and activism. Throughout my education and community work, I've engaged with many parts of Kentucky, from Frankfort to Boyle and Mercer counties.

In high school, I participated in the Kentucky Youth Assembly, which was a valuable experience for developing my leadership and advocacy skills. This program, along with the Kentucky United Nations Assembly, connected me to peers across the state who cared about social and environmental justice. It was part of my early involvement with the YMCA and community activism.

I took part in the Kentucky United Nations Assembly during high school, which broadened my understanding of global issues and diplomacy. This program was linked with the YMCA and complemented my participation in the Kentucky Youth Assembly. These experiences were important in shaping my commitment to social and environmental justice.

The YMCA was a key organization in my youth, providing opportunities like the Kentucky Youth Assembly and Kentucky United Nations Assembly. Through the YMCA, I connected with other young people passionate about social and environmental causes. It played a significant role in my development as an activist and community member.

I was fortunate to participate in the Governor's Scholars Program during high school, which allowed me to meet people from across Kentucky who cared deeply about social and environmental justice. This program was influential in expanding my network and understanding of statewide issues. It was a formative part of my educational journey.

I attended Centre College where I majored in environmental studies and Spanish and minored in Latin American studies. My time there included studying abroad in Mérida, Yucatán, and Antigua Guatemala, which enriched my understanding of Latin American culture. I was also involved in music ensembles and internships that connected me to community and environmental work.

During college, I studied abroad in Mérida, Yucatán, which was a profound experience that deepened my appreciation for Latin American culture and language. This time abroad complemented my academic focus on Spanish and Latin American studies. It also influenced my later community work and artistic expression.

I spent part of my college study abroad in Antigua Guatemala, including time in rural areas, which gave me firsthand experience with diverse communities and cultures. This enriched my understanding of Latin America beyond the classroom. It was an important part of my education and personal growth.

I interned with The Nature Conservancy in Louisville, where I helped run their youth program and urban forestry initiatives. This experience allowed me to combine my interests in ecology, community engagement, and environmental education. It was a valuable part of my journey toward environmental activism.

I was involved with the Kentucky Music Ensemble during college, which was a crucial outlet for my musical and artistic expression. As an alumna, I even toured the Northeast and traveled to Japan with the ensemble. Music has always been a central part of my identity and community work.

I traveled to Japan as part of the Kentucky Music Ensemble alumni tour, which was an incredible cultural experience. This trip expanded my horizons and connected my musical interests with international community building. It was a memorable part of my artistic journey.

In 2020, I began a graduate program in Community and Leadership Development at the University of Kentucky, which I completed in 2022. The program was funded and mostly online due to the pandemic, allowing me to continue working as assistant director at Josephine Sculpture Park. UK was an important place for my academic growth and research.

I have worked as the assistant director at Josephine Sculpture Park, a role I maintained while completing my graduate studies at the University of Kentucky. This position allowed me to blend my interests in art, community, and leadership. The park is a creative space that supports my vision of interconnectedness with neighbors and the environment.

After college, I was awarded a Fulbright English teaching assistantship in Costa Rica for ten months. I chose Costa Rica to learn from their environmental education and public policies, hoping to bring insights back to Kentucky. The experience was eye-opening and helped me understand both Costa Rica and the United States from new perspectives.

I lived in Costa Rica for ten months through a Fulbright teaching assistantship, immersing myself in local schools and communities. I was fascinated by their approaches to environmental education, health care, and public policy, which influenced my personal and professional perspectives. Costa Rica's size and culture offered a unique comparison to Kentucky and the United States.

As an American, I have reflected deeply on the United States' approach to public affairs, especially after my time in Costa Rica with the Fulbright Program. Living abroad gave me new insights into our country's policies and international role. My work and activism are rooted in improving community and environmental justice here in the U.S.

Adrienne Maree Brown's work has been profoundly validating for me, especially her ideas about community as ecology and joy. Her books and blogs helped me articulate concepts I'd felt my whole life but struggled to express, particularly around resilience and interconnectedness. Her influence is evident in how I approach my art and community building today.
